---
title: "lab session 6"
author: "Vandana Brahmasa"
date: "2025-04-10"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

Investment and Inflation
In the 70’s, did investment in stocks beat the inflation in the United States?
This is an open-ended research question. Please use what you have learned in this class to provide the
analysis.

```{r}
library(quantmod)
library(xts)
```
```{r}
#get data- S&P500 for investment and seasonal adjusted CPI for inflation
sp500 <- getSymbols("^GSPC", from = "1970-01-01", to = "1980-12-31", auto.assign = FALSE)
getSymbols("CPIAUCSL", src = "FRED")
```
```{r}
#Convert S&P to monthly format and use the closing prices for analysis
sp500_monthly <- to.monthly(sp500, indexAt = "lastof", OHLC = FALSE)
sp500_close <- Cl(sp500_monthly)
```


```{r}
#subset both data to the 70s
cpi_70s <- CPIAUCSL["1970/1979"]
sp500_70s <- sp500_close["1970/1979"]
head(cpi_70s)
head(sp500_70s)
```
```{r}
#normalize both data to start at 100 for easy comparison:
cpi_norm <- xts(coredata(cpi_70s) / as.numeric(first(cpi_70s)) * 100, order.by = index(cpi_70s))
sp500_norm <- xts(coredata(sp500_70s) / as.numeric(first(sp500_70s)) * 100, order.by = index(sp500_70s))
#explore structure of both datasets
head(cpi_norm)
tail(cpi_norm)
head(sp500_norm)
tail(sp500_norm)
#align cpi dates to match s&p date format to yyyy-mm-dd
index(cpi_norm) <- as.Date(as.yearmon(index(cpi_norm)), frac = 1)

```
```{r}
#merge the matching dates
combined <- merge(cpi_norm, sp500_norm, join = "inner")
colnames(combined) <- c("Inflation", "S&P500")
str(combined)

```
```{r}
plot(combined,
     main = "S&P 500 vs Inflation (1970s)",
     col = c("red", "blue"),
     ylab = "Indexed (Base = 100)")
legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Inflation", "S&P 500"),
       col = c("red", "blue"),
       lty = 1, lwd = 2)
```

As we can see, the red line indicating inflation is higher than the blue line indicating stock marker investments. This suggests that investments were unsuccessful in beating the inflation in the 70s.
